text: MiroFish is a next-generation AI prediction engine based on multi-agent technology. It constructs high-fidelity parallel digital worlds from seed information like breaking news, policy drafts, or financial signals. Thousands of agents with independent personalities, long-term memory, and behavioral logic freely interact and undergo social evolution. The system uses emergent collective intelligence rather than traditional forecasting. Built by Guo Hangjiang in 10 days, backed by $4.1M from Chen Tianqiao. 16K+ GitHub stars.

MiroFish Architecture Pipeline: 1) Graph Construction - extracts real-world seeds, injects individual/collective memories, implements GraphRAG. 2) Environment Setup - entity-relationship extraction, character persona generation, simulation parameter configuration. 3) Simulation Execution - dual-platform parallel simulation (Twitter-like and Reddit-like) with dynamic temporal memory updates. 4) Report Generation - ReportAgent interacts with post-simulation environment using diverse tool sets. 5) Deep Interaction - users dialogue with simulated individuals or the ReportAgent.

MiroFish agents possess: personality profiles generated during environment setup, long-term memory tracked through Zep Cloud integration, behavioral logic derived from seed information and graph structures, and social evolution capacity through emergent group behaviors from individual interactions. The simulation engine comes from OASIS (Open Agent Social Interaction Simulations) by CAMEL-AI, supporting up to one million agent interactions.

MiroFish Tech Stack: Frontend Vue.js, Backend Python 3.11-3.12, LLM Integration via OpenAI SDK-compatible APIs (tested with Alibaba Qianwen via DashScope), Memory Management via Zep Cloud, Graph Processing via GraphRAG, Simulation Engine OASIS by CAMEL-AI, Deployment via Docker. Required env vars: LLM_API_KEY, LLM_BASE_URL, LLM_MODEL_NAME, ZEP_API_KEY.

A-RAG (Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a framework that exposes hierarchical retrieval interfaces directly to the language model. Instead of designing algorithms for single-shot passage retrieval or predefining workflows, A-RAG provides three retrieval tools: keyword search, semantic search, and chunk read. The agent dynamically decides which tool to use at each step, enabling adaptive retrieval across multiple granularities. A-RAG consistently outperforms existing approaches with comparable or lower retrieved tokens.

A-RAG's three hierarchical retrieval tools: 1) Keyword Search - traditional term-based retrieval for precise matches. 2) Semantic Search - dense vector-based matching for conceptual similarity. 3) Chunk Read - fine-grained passage access for detailed information extraction. The system leverages frontier language models' reasoning and long-horizon tool-use capabilities to determine which retrieval tool best suits each query stage, replacing static retrieval workflows with dynamic adaptation.

The Compute-Efficient Frontier describes predictable scaling laws for LLM performance. Based on the 2020 paper 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models' by Kaplan et al., model performance follows a predictable power-law trend across model size, dataset size, and compute. However, we're approaching practical limits where further scaling yields insufficient value. The fundamental constraint is data scarcity - the threshold of all internet data being consumed. The Densing Law shows capability density doubles approximately every 3.5 months.

Evidence for diminishing returns in LLM scaling: frontier models show smaller performance jumps despite massive increases in training budgets. Long-horizon execution research shows that while benchmarks reveal genuine diminishing returns at the step level, they understate compounding benefits over long horizons. Frontier models' horizon length for software engineering tasks doubles every 7 months. The next AI breakthrough may come from smarter architectures, better training methods, and more efficient AI agents rather than bigger models.

When multi-agent systems help: Financial reasoning showed 80.9% improvement with centralized coordination. Tasks allowing simultaneous sub-task analysis benefit significantly from agent decomposition. When they hurt: Planning tasks degraded 39-70% across all multi-agent variants. Communication overhead fragments reasoning capacity for strictly ordered workflows. Five canonical architectures: Single-Agent, Independent, Centralized, Decentralized, Hybrid.

Agent System Scaling Principles: The Alignment Principle - parallelizable problems show dramatic gains when task decomposition matches agent capabilities. The Sequential Penalty - tasks requiring strict ordering suffer from coordination overhead. Tool-Coordination Trade-off - as tool count increases (16+), multi-agent coordination costs escalate disproportionately. Error amplification: Independent systems amplified errors 17.2x without peer validation, centralized orchestrators contained to 4.4x through validation checkpoints.

A trained predictive model achieved R-squared = 0.513, correctly identifying optimal agent architectures for 87% of unseen task configurations using measurable properties like tool density and decomposability. Architecture selection directly impacts deployment reliability. Papers on agentic and multi-agent systems skyrocketed from 820 in 2024 to over 2,500 in 2025.

Fine-tuning embedding models for RAG: Before tuning NDCG@10 = 0.5949, after tuning NDCG@10 = 0.8246, a 29.7 percentage point improvement (38% relative). Key to success: 1) Domain-specific training data capturing industry vocabulary. 2) MultipleNegativesRankingLoss positions similar texts closer, dissimilar texts farther apart. 3) Synthetic QA pair generation using LLMs from document chunks. 4) Track ndcg@10 during training with InformationRetrievalEvaluator.

Sentence Transformers v3 training best practices: Use BatchSamplers.NO_DUPLICATES for in-batch negatives with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss. Enable FP16 mixed precision. Use warmup_ratio=0.1 for stability. Combine eval_dataset for loss tracking with evaluator for task-specific metrics. For multi-dataset training use PROPORTIONAL sampler. Key evaluators: EmbeddingSimilarityEvaluator, TripletEvaluator, InformationRetrievalEvaluator.

Sentence Transformers loss functions: MultipleNegativesRankingLoss for (anchor, positive) pairs - best for retrieval. CoSENTLoss for pairs with similarity scores. SoftmaxLoss for classification. MatryoshkaLoss for variable-length embeddings. Multi-dataset training maps different loss functions to different datasets. Key datasets: all-nli (triplets), stsb (similarity 0-5), quora-duplicates (paraphrases), natural-questions (QA pairs).

Complete Sentence Transformers training pipeline: 1) Load model with SentenceTransformer. 2) Load datasets from HuggingFace Hub or local. 3) Define loss function matching data format. 4) Configure SentenceTransformerTrainingArguments with epochs, batch size, warmup, FP16. 5) Create evaluator (TripletEvaluator or InformationRetrievalEvaluator). 6) Create SentenceTransformerTrainer. 7) trainer.train(). 8) Save and push to Hub. Automatic model card generation included.
